borrower_criteria.other: Indigenous-led agriculture and agroforestry cooperatives in regions where borrowing options are limited. Governance must include Indigenous community leadership.

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: Supports Indigenous-led agriculture and agroforestry cooperatives in underserved regions where borrowing options are limited

impact.notes: CICAP cooperative supports 3,500+ Indigenous spice farmers; Emprocaucho supports 360+ smallholder rubber farmers

notable_organizations.name: CICAP
notable_organizations.description: Indigenous spice cooperative sourcing pepper, chili, pumpkin seeds and cardamom from 3,500+ farmer-members

notable_organizations.name: Emprocaucho
notable_organizations.description: Supports 360+ smallholder rubber farmers with affordable credit and working capital

notable_organizations.name: Undisclosed enterprise
notable_organizations.description: Third investment in Mexico; details not publicly disclosed

governance_and_partners.governance_model: Investment decisions approved by a five-member volunteer credit committee of Indigenous community leaders

context.summary: The Reciprocity Fund is an Indigenous-focused impact lending vehicle run by Beneficial Returns that provides flexible, low-cost working capital to agriculture and agroforestry cooperatives in Latin America. It intentionally targets regions where borrowing options are extremely limited. The fund operates on an explicitly non-extractive philosophy — its founder openly states investors should expect to lose money — and governance is held by Indigenous community leaders rather than external financiers.

notable_quotes.quote: We've been exploiting Indigenous people and their lands for too long. It's time to flip the dynamic and let the rest of the world lose a few bucks as a first and tiny step to correcting 500-plus years of abuse.

sources.title: Beneficial Returns makes three sustainable forestry and agriculture investments in Latin America

name: FINAPOP
legal_structure: Cooperative credit fund / solidarity economy fund

target_regions: Brazil — nationwide, including agrarian reform settlements

financial.investor_access: Open to any Brazilian citizen to invest savings directly into farmer cooperatives

borrower_criteria.other: Must be a cooperative, association, or agrarian reform settlement undergoing or committed to agroecology transition. Embedded in Brazil's MST and solidarity economy ecosystem.

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: Created explicitly to finance cooperatives of smallholder farmers transitioning to agroecology. Any Brazilian citizen can invest savings directly into agroecological farmer cooperatives.

impact.notes: 85 financings totaling ~$12M USD distributed to 53 cooperatives, associations, and agrarian reform settlements. Severe flooding in Rio Grande do Sul has caused significant crop and infrastructure losses among portfolio cooperatives.

notable_organizations.name: ACAP-PR
notable_organizations.description: Agrarian Reform Association of Paraná State; MST affiliate that helped secure a 30 million BRL (~$6M) state budget allocation for agroecology transition

governance_and_partners.governance_model: Cooperative structure; citizens invest directly into farmer cooperatives

context.summary: FINAPOP is a Brazilian cooperative credit fund created in 2021 in response to pandemic-driven food insecurity. It is unique in allowing any Brazilian citizen to invest their savings directly into smallholder farmer cooperatives undergoing an agroecology transition. Deeply embedded in Brazil's agrarian reform movement, it has reached 25,000 families across 53 cooperatives. It faces growing climate vulnerability — severe flooding in Rio Grande do Sul has caused significant crop and infrastructure losses among portfolio cooperatives — prompting calls for a crisis-resilience mechanism.

notable_quotes.quote: In Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil, there is a serious crisis with flooding in the entire state, and many cooperatives have lost their areas and their structures. We need a fund that can cover these moments of crisis and losses.

sources.title: How to Finance an Agroecology Transition: Innovations in Accessible and Affordable Credit Systems
Philanthropic Investment in Agroecology for a More Equitable Food Systems

name: NESsT Lirio Fund
legal_structure: Impact debt fund / SME loan fund

financial.interest_rate: Competitive; more flexible terms than traditional capital sources
financial.investor_access: Institutional and mission-aligned investors; Mercy Partnership Fund noted as an investor

min_annual_revenue_usd_by_source.context: NESsT Lirio Fund criteria page (general eligibility)
Colombia 2025 call for proposals; NESsT notes flexibility for enterprises near this threshold

borrower_criteria.min_annual_revenue_note: Threshold appears to vary by country and has increased over time. Brazil used a lower bar ($200K) to attract a new market at launch. Current standard for Colombia is $400K–$500K. Verify directly with NESsT for the applicable threshold.
borrower_criteria.min_years_operation: 2–3 years depending on country and cycle
borrower_criteria.other: Preference for enterprises aligned with organic, Fair Trade, or Rainforest Alliance standards. Must create dignified employment for vulnerable communities. Microfinance institutions and companies that lend to third parties are excluded. Application process takes 3–5 months.

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: Targets SMEs in sustainable agriculture and fishing, eco-tourism in protected areas, and inclusive employment. Preference given to enterprises aligned with organic, Fair Trade, or Rainforest Alliance standards.

impact.notes: Data as of December 2025 annual report. 30% of people reached are women. Net write-offs: 0.0%. Six companies including COAS, Greenbox, and Pebani have received repeat loans.

notable_organizations.description: Works with farmers in native communities in the Peruvian rainforest to produce medicinal plants and raw natural products; received repeat loan in 2025

notable_organizations.name: Greenbox
notable_organizations.description: Strengthens rural livelihoods through sustainable agriculture; received repeat investment

notable_organizations.name: Alto Kivinaki
notable_organizations.description: Producer of organic oranges

notable_organizations.name: Cotton Nation
notable_organizations.description: Organic cotton company working closely with 3,000+ Indigenous and rural producers, paying premium prices and driving regenerative farming

notable_organizations.name: Laguna Coffee Cooperative (Lagunas de los Condores)
notable_organizations.description: Coffee cooperative promoting gender equity; received repeat loan in 2025

notable_organizations.name: Cooperativa Agraria Cafetalera Santa Ana
notable_organizations.description: Coffee cooperative in Junín with ~170 farmers, nearly half women and over half aged 45+. NESsT extended a revolving working credit line in 2024 to pay producers during coffee campaigns.

notable_organizations.name: Cooperativa dos Agricultores do Vale do Amanhecer (COOPAVAM)
notable_organizations.description: Brazil nut producer cooperative in Mato Grosso; women-led, sources from 300 Indigenous producers, supports conservation of 1 million+ hectares of protected land

notable_organizations.name: Cattleya
notable_organizations.description: Flower producer improving livelihoods of rural women in Cundinamarca

governance_and_partners.governance_model: Locally-based loan managers with deep market knowledge collaborate with acceleration teams in Brazil, Colombia, and Peru. In-country portfolio teams provide one-on-one financial management support.

context.summary: The NESsT Lirio Fund is an impact debt fund launched in 2018, targeting small and medium-sized enterprises in the Andes-Amazon region of Latin America. As of December 2025 it manages $13.2M in assets with $9.1M in loans committed and outstanding across 21 businesses. It has disbursed over $20M through 37 loans since inception, preserving over 1.5 million hectares of land. Loan sizes range from $100,000 to $500,000. Borrower eligibility thresholds vary by country and program cycle, ranging from $200,000 to $500,000 in minimum annual revenue. The fund combines patient debt capital with NESsT's enterprise acceleration expertise and targets enterprises creating dignified employment for vulnerable communities in sustainable agriculture, fishing, and eco-tourism.

notable_quotes.quote: Reaching $20 million in disbursements shows both the growing demand for the type of patient capital we offer and the potential of enterprises working in critical ecosystems.
notable_quotes.attribution: Jorge Chavez, Lirio Fund Andes Director

notable_quotes.quote: Unlike traditional investors, we offer long-term, more patient capital to support our portfolio enterprises to grow their operations sustainably and maintain strong social and environmental impact over time.
notable_quotes.attribution: Ana Belen Jalixto Romero, Lirio Fund Andes Portfolio Manager

sources.title: The NESsT Lirio Fund Disburses Over $20 Million USD
NESsT Lirio Fund Expands into Brazil

financial.investor_access: Institutional; WRI Africa, US DFC, and Bezos Earth Fund are anchor investors

borrower_criteria.other: Targets the 'missing middle' — early-stage companies receiving less than $500,000. Must be building climate solutions including land restoration, regenerative agriculture, or renewable energy in Sub-Saharan Africa. Works with incubators and accelerators to identify investment-ready startups.

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: Focuses on nature-based solutions including land restoration and regenerative agriculture. One-third of portfolio explicitly dedicated to restoration investments. Broader climate mandate also includes renewable energy.

impact.notes: Portfolio targets AFR100 goal to restore 100 million hectares of degraded African land by 2030. Uses WRI TerraMatch platform for standardized impact monitoring. Fund has a 12-year lifespan; detailed restoration targets not yet publicly disclosed.

notable_organizations.name: Addax
notable_organizations.description: Restores degraded lands in Niger and generates revenue from gum arabic

notable_organizations.name: Association Mines Sans Pauvreté
notable_organizations.description: Restores wooded habitats of endangered chimpanzees while supporting sustainable livelihoods

governance_and_partners.governance_model: Fund led by and for African women. Works with incubators and accelerators to identify high-impact startups and provides investment-readiness training.

context.summary: Barka Fund is an early-stage impact venture fund and accelerator founded in 2020, focused on African entrepreneurs building climate solutions in Sub-Saharan Africa — with particular focus on the highly underserved Sahelian countries. The fund is led by and for African women, and targets the 'missing middle' of early-stage companies receiving less than $500,000. One-third of the portfolio is dedicated to land restoration, alongside sustainable agriculture and renewable energy. It uses WRI's TerraMatch platform for standardized impact monitoring. In January 2025 WRI Africa announced a $4M investment, complementing $8M from the US DFC and seed funding from the Bezos Earth Fund, bringing total capital raised to $12M.

notable_quotes.quote: Barka Fund, as a fund led by and for African women, exemplifies the leadership and innovation needed to restore degraded landscapes on the continent.

sources.title: WRI Africa Invests $4 Million in Barka Fund to Propel African Land Restoration
WRI backs Barka Fund to invest in locally-led land restoration in Africa

name: Pachamama Coffee Cooperative
manager: Farmer-owned; board composed entirely of farmer-member representatives

financial.investor_access: Not applicable. Consumer-facing cooperative — revenue flows directly to farmer-owners. Not open to outside investors.

borrower_criteria.other: Not applicable. Membership is open to qualifying farmer cooperatives; member cooperatives must be farmer-owned and committed to organic and regenerative practices.

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: Fully certified organic, regenerative agriculture-oriented cooperative. Indigenous Andean farming traditions inform the cooperative's approach to farming in balance with nature.

impact.notes: 400,000+ smallholder farmer-owners across 5 countries. 100% of profits returned to farmer-owners. Farmers own the roastery, brand, cafés, and employ 50+ US staff.

notable_organizations.name: COCLA
notable_organizations.description: Founding member cooperative from Quillabamba; led formation of Pachamama in 2006

notable_organizations.name: Manos Campesinas
notable_organizations.description: Represents 1,100 families; board member since 2013

notable_organizations.name: Prodecoop
notable_organizations.description: Member cooperative with active gender equity programs for women farmers

notable_organizations.name: La Union Regional
notable_organizations.description: Member cooperative based in Huatusco

notable_organizations.name: OCFCU
notable_organizations.description: Represents thousands of Ethiopian coffee farmers as Pachamama board member

governance_and_partners.governance_model: Board of directors entirely composed of farmer-members. One seat per country (Peru, Nicaragua, Guatemala, Mexico, Ethiopia). 100% of equity owned by farmer members.

context.summary: Pachamama Coffee Cooperative is a 100% farmer-owned global cooperative established in 2006. What distinguishes it from typical farmer cooperatives — most of which are member-owned at the farm level — is that Pachamama's farmer-members own the entire downstream US value chain: the roastery in Sacramento, the Pachamama brand, five cafés, and the employment of 50+ US staff. Most farmer cooperatives sell their product to a separate roaster or distributor who captures the majority of the margin. Pachamama was explicitly designed to close that gap. All profits flow to 400,000+ farmer-owners across Peru, Nicaragua, Guatemala, Mexico, and Ethiopia. Its regenerative organic farming practices are rooted in Indigenous Andean traditions. Included here as a significant model for farmer-led agroecological enterprise with vertical ownership, rather than as a capital vehicle open to outside investment.

notable_quotes.quote: Pachamama Coffee's model is unique. First of all because the farmers are the owners of Pachamama Coffee. This not only generates wealth for our families and communities, but also provides dignity. We are the owners in the entire coffee chain, it is our product from our hands to the consumer.

sources.title: Pachamama Coffee — Farmer Owned, Always Organic

name: Neycha Accelerator & Fund
manager: SHONA Capital (loan fund); Biovision Foundation (accelerator capacity building)

financial.investor_access: Not open to public investors. Loan fund capital managed by SHONA Capital from separate financiers. Biovision Foundation funds accelerator operations and capacity building only — not the loan fund.

borrower_criteria.other: Must be a Ugandan or Kenyan enterprise explicitly aligned with the 13 FAO principles of agroecology. At least one founder must be Ugandan or Kenyan. Business must have at least 2 employees with at least one founder working full-time. Requires 3 years of audited financials. Maximum loan size is $50,000 in local currency or USD. Assessed using proprietary Business Agroecology Criteria Tool (B-ACT).

agroecology_alignment.mandate_description: The only revolving loan fund in East Africa exclusively supporting agroecological enterprises (AEEs) as defined by the 13 FAO principles of agroecology. Investment criteria require explicit alignment across the entire agricultural value chain.

impact.notes: Cohort 2 underway as of 2025. Average loan size USD $30,000 per enterprise. Designed as a stepping stone — overarching goal is to prepare agroecological enterprises (AEEs) for follow-on investment from larger impact investors, not to be a terminal capital provider.

notable_organizations.name: Kaya Nuts
notable_organizations.description: Agroecological enterprise led by Miswaleh Zingizi; highlighted as a portfolio success story

key_funders: Biovision Foundation (accelerator operations and capacity building only; loan fund capital is from separate financiers managed by SHONA Capital)
governance_and_partners.governance_model: Independently managed by SHONA Capital for the loan fund. Biovision funds the set-up, operation, and capacity building of the accelerator. A steering committee drawn from both organizations oversees overall direction.

context.summary: The Neycha Accelerator & Fund is a joint initiative of Biovision Foundation (Swiss) and SHONA Capital (East Africa). It is the only revolving loan fund in East Africa exclusively targeting agroecological enterprises (AEEs) — businesses explicitly operating under the 13 FAO principles of agroecology. It provides patient capital of $10,000–$50,000 per enterprise to growth-stage businesses in Uganda and Kenya, combined with structured business acceleration. The governance structure is intentionally divided: Biovision's funds support only the accelerator setup and capacity building, while the loan fund capital is managed separately by SHONA Capital. Its goal is to prepare AEEs for larger follow-on investment rather than serve as a terminal capital provider.

sources.title: Neycha: The First Agroecology Accelerator and Fund
Neycha — helping entrepreneurs build flourishing businesses
